concerns.text: The plan proposes using 'Majority-Vote Spatial Overlap' to map high-resolution nodes (Schaefer-400) to low-resolution nodes (AAL-90) for Spearman rank correlation (FR-005). This creates a circular validation: multiple distinct high-res nodes will map to the *same* low-res node. Calculating a rank correlation between a set of 400 values and a set of 90 values (where 300+ values are collapsed into 90) is mathematically ill-defined and statistically invalid. The 'independent' predictor (high-res rank) is deterministically derived from the same spatial volume as the outcome (low-res rank), violating the independence of validation targets. The analysis cannot distinguish between 'true biological consistency' and 'artifactual correlation due to spatial averaging'.

The permutation test design (FR-006) proposes permuting 'spatially-mapped labels' to generate a null distribution. If the mapping is many-to-one (as required for 400->90), permuting the mapped labels does not break the spatial dependency between the two atlases; it merely shuffles the aggregated values. This fails to generate a valid null hypothesis for 'chance overlap' because the spatial structure (the fact that Schaefer nodes are subdivisions of AAL nodes) remains fixed. A valid null for cross-parcellation stability requires permuting the *subject* labels or the *time-series* data, not the node labels after a deterministic spatial collapse.

The plan defines hubs as the top 10% of nodes (`floor(N * 0.10)`). For AAL-90, this yields 9 hubs; for Schaefer-400, 40 hubs. Comparing the Jaccard index of a set of 9 items against a set of 40 items (after spatial mapping) is highly sensitive to the definition of the 'hub' set size. The plan acknowledges this in FR-008 (sensitivity analysis) but the primary analysis (FR-004) relies on a fixed 10% threshold. In connectomics, hub definitions are notoriously unstable at low N (90 nodes) where a single node change drastically alters the set. The plan does not account for the statistical power loss inherent in comparing sets of vastly different cardinalities (9 vs 40) without a normalized metric or a resampling strategy to equalize set sizes.
